stem: A retail study measures customer satisfaction score across 5 chains, each containing multiple stores. chain A has 15 observations with mean 39.3 /10 (within-chain SD = 6.4). chain B has 5 observations with mean 35.9 /10 (within-chain SD = 6.2). chain C has 15 observations with mean 59.7 /10 (within-chain SD = 8.4). chain D has 8 observations with mean 47.7 /10 (within-chain SD = 3.3). chain E has 10 observations with mean 53.6 /10 (within-chain SD = 7.5). The between-chain standard deviation is uncertain, estimated between 7.0 and 17.4 /10. Using a hierarchical Bayesian model, estimate the true customer satisfaction score for chain C, accounting for shrinkage toward the grand mean and the uncertainty in the between-chain variation.

ground_truth.answer: Hierarchical estimate for chain C: 59.3 /10 (shrunk from raw 59.7). Range [58.7, 59.5] as between-chain SD varies from 7.0 to 17.4. Pooled mean: 48.7.

solution_steps: Compute pooled (grand) mean: 48.7 /10 (weighted average of 5 chain means).
Shrinkage factor for chain C: B = σ²_between / (σ²_between + σ²_within/15). At σ_between=11.6: B = 0.966.
Interval [58.7, 59.5] captures uncertainty from the unknown between-group variance.
common_failure_modes: Using the raw observed mean (59.7) without shrinkage
Using only the grand mean (48.7) and ignoring group-specific data
Not recognizing that small-sample groups should be shrunk more toward the grand mean
Treating the between-group variance as known rather than uncertain
